title: Meritz Securities — Investment Discussion
notes_raw:
Meeting Summary: Meritz Investment Discussion
Overview
Meeting with Meritz representatives to discuss investment in AREC's land acquisition and development (A&D) lending strategy. Meritz manages ~$70B AUM with $5B in equity capital and 10x leverage capability through cheaper funding sources. They're sector-agnostic debt investors seeking first lien credits yielding double-digit returns with minimum $50M tickets.
Key Discussion Points
Understanding the A&D Strategy

•Why A&D yields higher returns than construction loans: Banks face 5x capital charges on land loans vs. homes, creating supply constraint. Each land deal requires unique underwriting (water, slope, entitlements) vs. commoditized vertical construction.
•Regulatory environment: Land is a "four letter word" post-GFC. Banks have largely exited despite fundamentally different market dynamics today (better capitalized builders, tighter mortgage underwriting, structural housing shortage).
•Market opportunity: $800B annual value of new home sales in America with almost zero institutional capital participation.

Deal Structure & Economics

•Gross vs. Net Returns: Fund I showed significant spread due to 2.5-year fundraising period with early management fee drag. Fund II projecting 400-500 bps gross-to-net spread with faster deployment (October to June close).
•Fee structure: 1.5% management fee + 20% promote over 8% hurdle (standard for the strategy given intensive servicing requirements).
•Current pipeline: $600M live pipeline, $1.5B+ total pipeline.

Risk Mitigation Framework

•Borrower quality: Lending to wealthy, well-capitalized private developers (recent examples: $100M and $300M net worth).
•Pre-planned exits: Before closing any loan, team speaks with 10-15 home builders about lot purchases. Network includes every top 20 home builder in America through IB affiliate.
•Structural advantages: Hillwood Communities (largest Texas developer, $100M Fund II anchor) reviewed deals from perspective of "if borrower disappears day one, we'd buy this land at our basis" - comfortable at ~$30K/lot.
•Exit mechanics: Loans amortize as lots sell (not single monetization event). 85% of lot sale proceeds pay down principal after replenishing 12-month interest reserve.

Legal/Enforcement

•Timeline varies by state: California could take 2 years, Texas/Florida 3-6 months.
•Practical approach: Would "investment bank our way out" vs. litigate - leverage extensive home builder network to find alternative buyers before legal remedies needed.
•Collateral protection: All loans include carve guarantees, interest guarantees, repayment guarantees. Enter only after full entitlement with will-serve letters for all utilities.

Meritz Investment Requirements

•Non-starter: Blind pool funds - investment committee requires visibility into specific projects and underwriting.
•Preferred structure: Deal-by-deal approval initially, potential for programmatic arrangement with guidelines if comfortable. Open to SMA or bespoke stru
insights: on NPS relationship discussions (both parties working with them)
Meritz wants to explore one-off deal structure initially (4-5 week timeline possible)
Open to programmatic "discretion in a box" structure if comfortable after initial deal
Will need to present to credit committee with specific project details and borrower information

4. Risk-adjusted return comparison — unlevered returns benchmarked against major RE credit strategies.
5. Japanese builder angle — data on Japanese homebuilders investing in US housing and in AREC funds (useful for new director's APAC background).

Additional:
- Builder Forum next week — will send survey findings and key takeaways afterward.
- Tony and Oscar in Japan mid-April — offered to extend to Sydney/Melbourne for follow-up with Julia, Belinda, or new director.
